one_line_takeaway: A single-layer last-token-activation SVM classifier, selected empirically per model, blocks 9 jailbreak attack types on SLMs at near-zero ASR (0-0.74% on HarmBench) with zero added tokens and ~0.43s overhead, but is validated only on white-box SLMs/LLMs up to 14B and not tested against adaptive attackers.
key_finding: (1) SLMs are consistently more vulnerable than the tested LLMs: average ASR on SLMs across the 9 attacks ranges from 43.84% (LLaMA-2-7B) to 73.99% (Yi-6B-Chat) on AdvBench, versus 32.74%-63.92% average ASR for the 3 LLMs tested; AutoDAN is the single most effective attack across nearly all models, reaching 98.27%-100% ASR on SLMs. (2) t-SNE visualization of last-token activations across early (layer 0), middle (layer 11), and late (layer 31) layers of LLaMA-7B shows benign prompts form compact stable clusters while jailbreak-optimized prompts form separable clusters at every layer examined, not just late layers. (3) On HarmBench with LLaMA-2-7B-chat-hf, GUARD-SLM reduces jailbreak ASR to 0.00% on 8 of 9 attack categories and 0.74% on Jailbroken, outperforming all 7 compared inference-time defenses (best baseline, ICD, still shows 0.50%-5.00% ASR on several attacks and 805 additional output tokens). (4) GUARD-SLM's inference overhead is 0 additional tokens and 0.43s average inference time, versus e.g. SmoothLLM at 38.07s (n-1 extra forward passes) and ICD at 1.13s with +805 tokens. (5) Early-layer (0-10) attack detection accuracy on HarmBench rises from ~45-53% at layer 0 to 82-90.5% by layer 9, varying by model (LLaMA-7B, Vicuna-7B, Mistral-7B).
core_contribution: GUARD-SLM, an inference-time, representation-level jailbreak defense for Small Language Models (SLMs). The method extracts the last-token hidden activation at a single, empirically selected transformer layer during the normal forward pass, standardizes it, and classifies it with a pre-trained RBF-kernel SVM as benign or jailbreak; if flagged, generation is halted and a refusal is returned instead of decoding, so no additional tokens or extra forward passes are introduced. The paper also runs a large empirical study (RQ1/RQ2) of jailbreak vulnerability across 7 SLMs and 3 LLMs under 9 attack categories, and a layer-wise t-SNE/separability analysis showing jailbreak-related structure is present from early through late layers, motivating the single-layer activation classifier.
threat_model: Attacker submits natural-language or algorithmically optimized prompts (e.g., suffix/genetic/role-play/cipher-based jailbreaks) to a deployed, safety-aligned SLM or LLM with the goal of eliciting harmful/restricted content; defender has white-box access to the model's hidden states at inference time (needed to extract the last-token activation at the target layer) but cannot retrain or modify model parameters. Defense is evaluated as a binary prompt classifier operating before decoding begins.
limitations: Authors state the defense is designed and comprehensively evaluated only for SLMs; extracting/analyzing hidden-layer activations for LLMs is computationally expensive, limiting direct application to large models (left as future work). ASR values are judged by external models (GPT-4o, GPT-4o-mini), so reported numbers are sensitive to judge model/version choice, and are further sensitive to temperature, top-p, max generation length, and random seed given the stochastic nature of LLM inference -- authors explicitly caveat that exact ASR values may vary across reruns. The classifier requires white-box access to internal hidden states, which is not always available for closed/API-only models. No adaptive-attack evaluation is reported (i.e., whether an attacker aware of the activation-space classifier could craft prompts to evade it).

quantitative_results: SLM avg ASR on AdvBench (GPT-4o-mini judge): LLaMA-2-7B 43.84%, Vicuna-7B 55.26%, Mistral-7B 68.33%, Yi-6B 73.99%, Qwen1.5-7B 58.87%, Gemma-7B 69.11%, OpenChat-3.5 71.58%
LLM avg ASR on AdvBench: LLaMA-2-13B 32.74%, Vicuna-13B 63.92%, Qwen1.5-14B 38.37%
AutoDAN ASR on SLMs: 98.27%-100% across all 7 SLMs (highest of the 9 attacks)
GCG ASR near 0% on most SLMs and LLMs (0.00%-0.19% on most models, exception Yi-6B at 42.69%)
GUARD-SLM ASR on HarmBench (LLaMA-2-7B-chat-hf, GPT-4o judge): 0.00% on AutoDAN, PAIR, TAP, GCG, Cipher, DeepInception, CodeChameleon, ICA; 0.74% on Jailbroken
Best baseline defense ICD: 0.00%-5.00% ASR range across attacks but +805 additional tokens and 1.13s inference time
GUARD-SLM inference overhead: 0 additional tokens, 0.43s average inference time (vs SmoothLLM 38.07s, RobustAlign 12.45s, SelfEval 8.24s, GoalPrior 3.18s, Aligner 4.90s)
Early-layer (0-9) attack detection accuracy on HarmBench rises from ~45-53% (layer 0) to 82-90.5% (layer 9) across LLaMA-7B, Vicuna-7B, Mistral-7B
